topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The heavy oak door clicked shut with a finality that felt like a physical blow. I stood in the echoing silence of his office, the scent of aged paper and stale coffee suddenly suffocating. "You're gone?" The words scraped out of my throat, sounding brittle and sharp even to my own ears. I slammed my palm onto the mahogany desk, the vibration rattling the framed photographs. He was just… gone. No note, no final conversation, just an empty chair where the bedrock of my career used to sit. My jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. I took a ragged breath, trying to steady the tremor in my hands, but the anger was a hot, suffocating thing rising in my chest, demanding an outlet, a reckoning.

[story 2]

I stared at the framed plaque on the wall—the one commemorating his thirty years of service. It seemed mocking now, a monument to a departure I hadn't been invited to witness. Every muscle in my neck was tight, pulling my head down until my chin nearly touched my sternum. "Seriously?" I managed, the sound low and dangerously controlled. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring through a film of sudden, stinging moisture on my cheeks. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a profound sense of being dismissed, of having my entire trajectory abruptly derailed by a casual vanishing act. I turned back, my eyes flashing with a cold, hard intensity. I needed to know *why*, and I needed him to answer, even if the answer was just another layer of infuriating silence.

[story 3]

The silence in the hallway was deafening. I kept replaying the last exchange, searching for the cue, the subtle shift that signaled this monumental abandonment. My fists were clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my forearms. When I finally found him in the reception area, his smile was too bright, too practiced. "Well, that's that," he chirped, waving a hand dismissively. I didn't move. I simply let the air drain from my lungs in a slow, deliberate exhale. "You didn't say anything," I stated, my voice flat, devoid of inflection. The temperature in the room seemed to drop. I watched him, cataloging every slight twitch of his composure, my entire being radiating a focused, simmering displeasure that threatened to boil over. I wouldn't let this pass without a forceful confrontation.
